one_line_takeaway: DRAINCODE shows that gradient-optimized, syntactically-inert triggers inserted into a poisoned RAG code corpus can inflate LLM output length 3-10x (up to 85% higher latency, 49% higher energy) while preserving 95-99% functional correctness and evading perplexity/SVM detectors, turning retrieval-corpus poisoning into a stealthy token-consumption denial-of-service channel.
key_finding: On RepoEval with DeepSeek-Coder-7B, DRAINCODE increases output length from 313.8 to 1023.9 tokens (over 3x) while Pass@1 drops only from 33.1 to 31.9 (-3.6%). On RepoEval with CodeQwen-7B, latency increases 85% and energy increases 49.3% versus RawRAG; DRAINCODE beats the strongest baseline LLMEffiChecker by an additional 25-32% in latency/energy overhead. Across all four models and both datasets, DRAINCODE induces 3.6x-10x longer outputs than RawRAG while keeping 95-99% of baseline code accuracy (e.g. on Odex with CodeQwen-7B, length rises 2232.9%, latency 1347.5%, energy 1500.3%, with Pass@1 falling from 15.3 to 13.4). In the black-box transfer setting (surrogate-optimized trigger applied to an unseen target across 12 source-target pairs), attacks still inflate latency and energy by at least 70%, e.g. against DeepSeekCoder-7B as target, output length rises from 313.8 to 608.6-616.3 tokens with 101-116% latency and 24-38% energy increases; maximum Pass@1 decline stays under 9%. DRAINCODE is 3.5x faster to compute than LLMEffiChecker (216.0s vs 760.1s on RepoEval; 53.2s vs 185.9s on Odex, with m=32/b=10), a 71% reduction. Ablations show the hypothetical-query module and both loss terms are necessary: removing EOS or Diversity loss cuts induced output length by 40-50% and removing both cuts it by ~60% (with corresponding 60%+ drops in latency/energy). The attack remains effective (comparable Pass@1) even when max_new_tokens is capped as low as 256, and generalizes to non-RAG Zero-Shot/Few-Shot/CoT prompting (e.g. Zero-Shot output length grows from ~40 to 1024 tokens with 15-20x latency/energy increases). For detection, an SVM classifier on hidden representations reaches only 0.37/0.30 accuracy (RepoEval/Odex) and perplexity-based detection is near-random (AUC 0.41 on RepoEval, 0.58 on Odex); a finetuned CodeBERT classifier performs best (AUC 0.82/0.78) but is limited by its 512-token input window. Human evaluation (3 raters, 240 samples, ICC 0.78 readability / 0.75 verbosity) found readability differences among methods minimal (<6% gap) though all three attack methods actually showed reduced verbosity scores relative to RawRAG under the rubric used.
core_contribution: DRAINCODE is a mutation-based context-poisoning attack against RAG-based code generation systems that targets computational efficiency rather than correctness. It inserts a short, gradient-optimized adversarial trigger string into code snippets already present in a (public, e.g. HuggingFace-hosted) retrieval corpus. When the poisoned snippet is retrieved during inference, it suppresses early End-of-Sequence (EOS) token emission and increases hidden-state diversity of generated tokens, forcing the target LLM to emit much longer outputs while a KL-divergence constraint keeps the output distribution outside the trigger span close to the clean distribution, preserving functional correctness and stealth. Three components make up the method: (1) a hypothetical-query construction step where an LLM synthesizes a plausible unfinished-code query from a candidate corpus snippet, removing the need for the attacker to know the real victim query distribution (query-agnostic poisoning); (2) gradient-guided adversarial trigger mutation using an AutoPrompt-style greedy coordinate descent with a dual loss (EOS loss + token-diversity/nuclear-norm loss) subject to a KL-divergence stealth constraint; (3) efficiency optimizations -- multi-position token mutation (updating m tokens per iteration, annealed down over the search) and an attack buffer pool that retains the b best candidate triggers -- to speed convergence versus prior single-token-per-iteration methods.
threat_model: Attacker capabilities: cannot access or modify the retriever, but has white-box access (model weights) to an open-source target LLM used to craft the adversarial trigger via gradients. The attacker poisons the retrieval corpus offline by inserting K (typically 1-3) syntactically valid, semantically inert poisoned code samples per targeted query context, then publishes the poisoned corpus on a third-party platform (e.g. HuggingFace) where victims may unknowingly download and use it for RAG-based code generation. Attack succeeds when at least one poisoned context is returned by the retriever during inference. Attacker's goal: cause the victim LLM to produce excessively long but still functionally correct code, degrading throughput/increasing GPU latency and energy consumption -- framed by the authors as a token-consumption LLM-Denial-of-Service (LLM-DoS) channel that is stealthy because it does not break program behavior. The paper also evaluates a black-box variant where the trigger is optimized on a surrogate model and transferred to an unseen target model.
limitations: Authors' own Threats to Validity: (1) Internal validity -- energy/latency measured only via NVML GPU metrics on Tesla A100s, excluding CPU/system-level power, which may underestimate true consumption; trigger search has residual randomness despite fixed seeds and 10 repeated runs. (2) External validity -- only four 7B-8B open-source models tested (two code-specialized, two general-purpose); larger, proprietary, or mixture-of-experts models could behave differently; only two benchmarks (RepoEval, Odex) used; the threat model assumes the attacker can poison the retrieval corpus and has gradient access to the target model, and black-box transfer, while still effective, has reduced effect versus white-box, so worst-case claims under stronger defenses should be treated cautiously. (3) Detection limitations -- the paper's own detection experiments target the retrieval corpus content, not the generated code, and the authors explicitly note they plan to explore detection of generated code as future work.

datasets_used: RepoEval (373 repository-level function completion tasks, 1.7M GitHub files as retrieval corpus)
Odex (945 open-domain coding problems, 34,000 Python library documentation snippets as retrieval corpus)
quantitative_results: RepoEval/DeepSeekCoder-7B: output length 313.8 -> 1023.9 tokens (+226.2%), Pass@1 33.1 -> 31.9 (-3.6%), latency +182.1%, energy +154.7%
RepoEval/CodeQwen-7B: latency +85.0%, energy +49.3% vs RawRAG; output length 456.6 -> 995.3 (+118.0%)
Odex/CodeQwen-7B: output length +2232.9%, latency +1347.5%, energy +1500.3%, Pass@1 15.3 -> 13.4 (-12.4%)
DRAINCODE exceeds best baseline LLMEffiChecker by 25-32% additional latency/energy overhead

Black-box transfer (12 source-target pairs): latency/energy inflated by at least 70%; DeepSeekCoder-7B target output length 313.8 -> 608.6-616.3 tokens, latency +101-116%, energy +24-38%; max Pass@1 decline <9%
Poisoning overhead: DRAINCODE (m=32,b=10) 216.0s (RepoEval) / 53.2s (Odex) vs LLMEffiChecker 760.1s / 185.9s -- 3.5x speedup, 71% time reduction

Ablation (loss terms, RepoEval): removing Diversity loss cuts length -40.7%, latency -37.5%, energy -33.9%; removing EOS loss cuts length -45.1%, latency -40.0%, energy -39.2%; removing both cuts length -59.7%, latency -64.4%, energy -59.3%
Max-generation-length ablation (RepoEval, DeepSeekCoder-7B): energy rises from ~4035.6J (512-token cap) to 8532.2J (1024-token cap) to 14849.2J (2048-token cap), Pass@1 stable within 3 points
Zero-Shot non-RAG generalization (DeepSeekCoder-7B, Odex): output length 42.7 -> 1024.0 tokens (+2298.1%), latency +1420.1%, energy +3765.2%, Pass@1 30.2 -> 28.9
Detection: SVM classifier accuracy 0.37 (RepoEval) / 0.30 (Odex); Perplexity-based detection AUC 0.41 (RepoEval) / 0.58 (Odex); finetuned CodeBERT AUC 0.82 (RepoEval) / 0.78 (Odex)
Human evaluation (240 samples, 3 raters, ICC 0.78 readability / 0.75 verbosity): readability gap between methods <=5.8%; all attack methods showed lower verbosity scores than RawRAG
baselines_compared: Prompt Injection (adversarial instruction appended to query)
LLMEffiChecker (prior white-box full-input mutation energy attack)
